summary: Joel announces the official shipping of FogBugz 6.0, highlighting its major new features including an integrated Wiki, API, overhauled search engine, and Ajax improvements. The centerpiece feature is Evidence-Based Scheduling (EBS), which uses Monte Carlo simulation to generate probability distributions for ship dates rather than single-point estimates. Instead of trusting developer estimates at face value, EBS looks at historical accuracy of each developer's past estimates to simulate multiple possible futures. The post doubles as a product announcement and a sales pitch with introductory pricing.
key_points: FogBugz 6.0 ships with an integrated Wiki, API, revamped search engine, and Ajax-driven UI improvements
Evidence-Based Scheduling uses Monte Carlo simulation (bootstrapping) to replace single ship dates with probability distribution curves
EBS works by comparing developers' past estimates against their actual delivery speeds, rather than taking estimates on faith
The system simulates 60 equally probable futures to generate a shipping probability curve
money_quotes: Instead of adding up everyone's estimates—instead of taking them on faith—FogBugz does a Monte Carlo simulation looking at what speeds developers worked at in the past, vis-à-vis their estimates.
What you get, instead of a date, is a probability distribution curve that shows the probability that the product will ship on such-and-such a date.
You use that same distribution of probabilities that you had in the past and run a simulation of 60 futures each of which will occur with equal probability.
EBS is interesting enough that I'll devote a whole article to explaining it as soon as I get a minute of free time.
Go make yourself a free online trial, what are you waiting for?
key_insight: Software schedule estimates become far more useful when treated as probability distributions derived from historical data rather than taken at face value.
